title: Device Swap Password Reset with Immediate High-Value Purchase
narrative: Attacker gains credentials through phishing or credential stuffing, then logs in from a device never associated with the account (device_match=0). Within 15-45 minutes, they trigger a password reset, update the email to a disposable domain (P_emaildomain: guerrillamail.com, tempmail.net), and complete a purchase of $850-$2,400 in electronics or gift cards. The purchase occurs from a different geographic location (dist1 > 500km from historical addr1) with shipping to a freight forwarder address. The attacker deliberately keeps TransactionAmt below the account's historical maximum by $50-$100 to avoid automatic hard blocks. Session duration from login to checkout is extremely compressed: 8-12 minutes versus the victim's typical 25+ minute browsing pattern. Card verification (CVV) passes because saved payment methods don't require re-entry, but AVS fails due to shipping address change.
indicators: device_id never seen in account history (device_match=0) combined with password reset within 45 minutes
P_emaildomain changed to disposable service (guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail) within same session
dist1 > 500km between historical addr1 and current transaction location
TransactionAmt $850-$2,400, positioned $50-$100 below account's historical max transaction
Session duration 8-12 minutes from login to purchase completion vs account average 25+ minutes
ProductCD = 'W' (electronics) or 'C' (gift cards) with R_emaildomain != historical shipping contact
AVS mismatch on shipping address despite CVV match on stored payment method
summary: A login from a never-before-seen device is followed within minutes by a password reset, email change to a disposable domain (guerrillamail, tempmail), and a rushed checkout (8-12 minutes vs. typical 25+) for high-value electronics or gift cards shipped 500+ km away from the account's normal location.
reasoning: The attacker is racing to extract maximum value before the legitimate owner notices, using stolen credentials to hijack the account, lock out the victim with new email/password, and ship goods to a drop address while deliberately staying just under transaction limits to avoid automatic fraud blocks.
indicator_explanations: Brand new device appears right after password gets reset
Account email suddenly switches to a disposable throwaway service
Transaction originates hundreds of miles from cardholder's usual location
Purchase amount deliberately stays just under account's historical spending ceiling
Attacker rushes through checkout far faster than legitimate account owner
High-risk items shipped to email address that doesn't match account history
Shipping address fails verification while stored card details pass security
